title: Trump says US strikes hit Iran in 'honour' of American soldiers killed
description: Elsewhere in the region, Kuwait's armed forces said they had intercepted Iranian drones.On the US strikes, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency rep
body: Elsewhere in the region, Kuwait's armed forces said they had intercepted Iranian drones.On the US strikes, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that several cities had been hit including Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini.There were also Iranian state media reports that two oil tankers had exploded as the vessels tried to cross the strait.Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said the tankers were going through what it called an "unsafe and hazardous southern route" of the strait and "exploded and were brought to a halt". Centcom disputed the claims. The IRGC added that the strait "will not be safe for the transit of petrochemical products, nor even a single drop of oil and gas" as long as the US strikes continue and that it will respond with a "punitive operation".Over the weekend, the US confirmed that a service member had been killed after an Iranian attack in northern Iraq on Saturday.That news came after the US said a separate attack on a base in Jordan on Friday had killed two soldiers there. The two have now been identified by the Pentagon as Pte Isabella Gonzales, 19, and Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25. A third service member was declared missing after the attack in Jordan, and US officials say "unidentified remains" have been found during the search process. As the strikes continued over the weekend, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said that the US "always remains open to a diplomatic solution".He also said "the world has to decide whether or not it's going to allow an international waterway to be under the control" of Iran, saying Tehran was using the crucial shipping route as leverage.His comments come as a preliminary deal made in mid-June to stop fighting has unravelled, with negotiations for a permanent peace making little progress after Trump declared the agreement was "over" on 8 July.

schema.headline: Trump says US strikes hit Iran in 'honour' of American soldiers killed

title: Rare identical quadruplets born in Australia
description: An Australian woman has given birth to rare, naturally-conceived identical quadruplets after a pregnancy described as "extremely high risk" by the Roy
body: An Australian woman has given birth to rare, naturally-conceived identical quadruplets after a pregnancy described as "extremely high risk" by the Royal Brisbane and Women's Hospital.The four girls, who were delivered via caesarean section at 28 weeks and four days on 14 July, were monozygotic quadruplets - where a single fertilised egg splits into four embryos.The hospital said the girls' mother, 34-year-old Jenitar Na'amoana, and her husband Jortham, already had four children and were taken by "immense surprise" when told to expect quadruplets.Dr Alexa Bendall, who cared for the mother from the start of her pregnancy, estimated identical quadruplets occur "at one in every 15 million pregnancies" and said "but to share the same placenta - this is unheard of".Bendall, who is a maternal foetal medicine specialist and obstetrician, said such a pregnancy was very high-risk, with the hospital admitting Na'amoana as an inpatient at 25 weeks so she could be monitored."We always said that if we could get her to 28 weeks, we would be doing well," Bendall said."So the fact that we've got four beautiful, healthy babies born at 28 weeks and four days is incredible and we're very happy to be a part of it," she added.

schema.headline: Rare identical quadruplets born in Australia

title: Ten killed as Russian attacks on merchant ships in Black Sea intensify
description: The death toll of a Russian strike on a cargo ship off the coast of the Ukrainian city of Odesa has risen to 10. The Guinea-Bissau-flagged ship was hi
body: The death toll of a Russian strike on a cargo ship off the coast of the Ukrainian city of Odesa has risen to 10. The Guinea-Bissau-flagged ship was hit by Russian cruise missiles on Sunday, the Ukrainian Air Force said, causing a fire.A search and rescue operation was launched and lasted all night. By Monday morning, Ukraine's seaports authority said nine crew members had been killed, as well as one of its pilots. India later said four of its citizens died in the attack.In recent weeks, the Odesa region has come under sustained attack. Twenty-eight people died in Russian strikes on the region in July alone, regional head Oleh Kiper told Ukrainian TV on Monday.Russian attacks on cargo ships in the Black Sea have also increased. Sunday's attack on the Guinea-Bissau-flagged Golden Leo ship was the deadliest of its kind. Among the victims were four Indian nationals, the country's foreign ministry said, adding that endangering crew members or impending freedom of navigation "is deplorable and should be avoided".Foreign-flagged ships like the Golden Leo exploit Ukraine's last remaining maritime export corridor to transport cargo down the Romanian and Bulgarian coastlines and onwards to Turkey. Maritime exports account for over two-thirds of Ukrainian agricultural exports, for a value of roughly $9bn (£6.7bn) - an essential source of revenue for Kyiv. Fighting in the Black Sea was at a stalemate for most of the four-and-a-half years since Moscow first launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine. While Ukrainian ships were unable to move further than the area surrounding Odesa due to Russian control of much of the coast, Kyiv's drones forced Moscow to keep its assets safely tucked in its naval bases in the eastern Black Sea.But Russia's ramping up of attacks against cargo ships is likely meant to deter foreign companies from buying Ukrainian products and using its Black Sea maritime corridor. On Sunday, Ukraine's Prime Minister Serhiy Koretskyi said his government and the military command were "preparing decisions that will enhance the safety of navigation and help protect civilian vessels and seafarers" from Russian attacks. Despite the constant shelling, Odesa remains a prime holiday destination for Ukrainians. Kiper said earlier this month that air raid shelters had been set up in 40 beaches in the region which were open to the public - but warned that seaside locations could be particularly dangerous because drones and missiles are often launched from the sea.For its part, Ukraine too has ramped up its attacks on Russian assets in the Black Sea and the Sea of Azov. On Monday, Ukraine's drone forces commander Robert Brovdi said 183 vessels had been struck in July. While this figure has not been independently verified, the BBC has seen evidence of damage to numerous vessels in both the Black Sea and the Sea of Azov on satellite.Elsewhere in the country, Russian strikes killed five people in separate attacks on eastern and southern Ukrainian cities.According to the UN, June saw the highest number of Ukrainian civilians killed and injured since April 2022.  At least 293 civilians were killed and 1,990 injured.For its part, Kyiv also continued to hit logistics facilities and an oil depot in Russia's Moscow region. Sergei Sobyanin, the mayor of the Russian capital, said Kyiv had launched 400 drones towards the region overnight on Monday, although most were shot down. Local authorities later said 10 people had been injured as a result.And local authorities in Russia's Belgorod border region said a Ukrainian drone strike on a bus had killed five people, including a child, on Monday. Ukraine has not yet commented.

schema.headline: Ten killed as Russian attacks on merchant ships in Black Sea intensify

title: Faisal Islam: Andy Burnham has big ideas - but what will they cost?
description: Up until this point, the new Prime Minister Andy Burnham has described his policy changes with a broad brush. From today, he faces the constraints, tr
body: Up until this point, the new Prime Minister Andy Burnham has described his policy changes with a broad brush. From today, he faces the constraints, trade offs, and realities of high national office.For example, Burnham has said Tuesday's announcements on cost of living support would be paid for. In other words, it would mean a tax rise or spending cut, so we will see rather quickly what the new PM prioritises when push comes to shove.Meanwhile, the markets seem to have already reacted to the suggestion he would be using some "flexibility" in his borrowing rules to help with new announcements. The UK government's 10-year borrowing rose above 5% on Monday after falling in recent days. It has not gone up in this way in other European economies.The move was not huge, but it shows the sensitivities at a moment when Burnham is overhauling his cabinet.The "flexibility" he was talking about is, I understand, about the treatment of financial institutions. This has occurred in some green energy policies and essentially helps exempt certain types of borrowing from the measures of debt. There is scope for this model to extend to housing and other infrastructure. It was not a general suggestion of, for example, using up the increased headroom against the government's borrowing rules.All this shows how every tricky decision and trade off is being watched by the country and the markets at the same time.Burnham's camp has also floated the idea of unfreezing the income tax threshold – and I'm taking it pretty seriously that this may happen.Some big unions have swung behind the idea, which would cost at least £4bn depending on the rate of inflation. And others have floated the idea of not just unfreezing the thresholds in 2027 – lifting it from the current £12,570 basic rate to £13,000 and at the upper end from £50,271 to £52,000 – but rolling it back to what it would have been if the freeze hadn't been implemented in 2022. This would be much more expensive to do.The UK is in the middle of a nine-year threshold freeze announced by then-chancellor Rishi Sunak to repay the billions borrowed to fund the Covid lockdown. The freeze was extended at the last Budget by Rachel Reeves until 2030-31, pushing one in six workers into the 40p bracket.It is intriguing that Burnham says this came up on Makerfield doorstep, as is the fact that significant unions now prioritise a policy that affects their members on or around £50,000 a year. We're talking the likes of nurses, midwives, paramedics, teachers, and engineers.However, as some current Number 10 insiders have pointed to, it adds to a list of net spending policies and raises questions about the extent of tax rises or extra borrowing required to make Budget sums add up.More widely, Burnham has also said he would publish a 10-year plan for the country and the economy later this year, likely to detail his wider plans on decentralisation, devolution, and rebuilding Britain. Ordinarily, you might expect a significant infrastructure programme with such plans, but Burnham also wants to fund the Defence Investment Plan, a multi-billion, multi-year spending commitment for the UK military. How will he square this?In addition, his first priority going through the doors, he said, would be a national plan to end rough sleeping. He made a similar pledge in Greater Manchester, but some early progress went in reverse in recent years. When I questioned him about this in February, he said more funding and powers from central government were needed to deal with the recent rise. As his first act as PM, he has now released funds to address it from within the housing budget. Long term, he said the answer is mass council house building. This will take years to feed through.Welfare is another critical test. Here, I'm told, Burnham expects to show he can make progress on what he called "sustainable reform". This will lean heavily on Alan Milburn's review, which is likely to recommend significant job support funds and mental health assistance for young workers in order to reduce welfare bills. This is likely to be delivered by local mayors who know what works in their regions. Burnham hopes to show the markets he can deliver the welfare reform that eluded former prime minister Sir Keir Starmer.This is the new PM's conundrum. He faces the same manifesto and borrowing rule constraints as his predecessor, yet he clearly wants to do more. He says his rewiring of Britain will help the economy grow, but many of these solutions could take years. And the recent rapid turnover of prime ministers and chancellors shows, even thinking a few years ahead might feel like a luxury.

schema.headline: Faisal Islam: Andy Burnham has big ideas - but what will they cost?

title: Ofcom opens investigation into Married at First Sight UK
description: Separately, in a letter to MPs in June, Ofcom said it would examine the findings of Channel 4's review into welfare on the show, and would "urgently"
body: Separately, in a letter to MPs in June, Ofcom said it would examine the findings of Channel 4's review into welfare on the show, and would "urgently" consider whether any further action is required.At the time, the watchdog called the allegations exposed by Panorama "shocking and deeply disturbing".Earlier this month, BBC News revealed that a cast member from the show had been arrested on suspicion of rape following the Panorama programme.The individual is not being named; nor is the alleged victim. Anyone who makes an allegation of rape is entitled to anonymity by law.Billed as a "bold social experiment", Married at First Sight UK - known to many as MAFS - sees single people agree to "marry" total strangers, after meeting for the first time at their mock weddings.The UK version, which has been running for 10 seasons, is one of Channel 4's biggest shows, with audience figures often topping three million.In May, Channel 4 pulled all episodes of the show from its streaming service just hours ahead of the transmission of the Panorama investigation.No decision has yet been made on whether to broadcast the latest series of the show, which has already been filmed.The broadcaster has commissioned a review into contributor welfare on the show, which is due to be finished later this summer. However, it only intends to publish "a summary of findings", rather than the full report.Lawyers for CPL, the independent production company that makes the UK version of the show, have previously said its welfare system is "gold standard" and industry-leading.If you have more information about this story, you can reach Noor by email at noor.nanji@bbc.co.uk

schema.headline: Ofcom opens investigation into Married at First Sight UK

title: Hamas names Khalil al-Hayya as new overall leader
description: The Palestinian armed group Hamas has elected Khalil al-Hayya as its new leader, according to a statement.He replaces Yahya Sinwar, who was killed by
body: The Palestinian armed group Hamas has elected Khalil al-Hayya as its new leader, according to a statement.He replaces Yahya Sinwar, who was killed by the Israeli military in Gaza in October 2024.Hayya, who already headed the movement in Gaza from exile, was widely seen as representing the more hardline faction that emerged from the devastating war with Israel that began almost three years ago. He was a close associate of Sinwar and is seen as continuing the political line going back to the late Ismail Haniyeh.As the chief negotiator for Hamas, Hayya gained international prominence by leading the group's delegations at indirect ceasefire talks, meeting mediators in Egypt and Qatar.He had recently taken on greater responsibility for Hamas's diplomatic and political strategies.In September 2025, the 65-year-old survived an Israeli air strike that targeted a meeting of Hamas leaders in the Qatari capital, Doha. Five lower-level members of the group, including one of his sons, were killed along with a member of Qatar's security forces.Israel's prime minister said at the time that it targeted the Hamas leaders because they had organised the attack on southern Israel on 7 October 2023, which triggered the Gaza war.Another two of Hayya's sons were killed in previous Israeli attempts on his life in Gaza, while a fourth was killed ​in an Israeli air strike in Gaza in May.Hayya's election as head of Hamas's political bureau followed two rounds of voting, with members apparently split between competing visions for the future direction of the group.The first round was inconclusive, with former political bureau chief Khaled Meshaal narrowly winning more votes than Hayya, but failing to pass the required threshold, according to a well-informed Palestinian official familiar with the election.Under Hamas's internal rules, a candidate must secure at least 36 votes from the 69-member electoral college to be elected outright.It is understood that the second round run-off was decided by a first-past-the-post vote.Hamas is going through one of the most difficult periods in its history.The group - which is designated as a terrorist organisation by Israel, the UK, the US and many other countries - has been severely weakened by the Gaza war, during which much of its senior military and political leadership has been killed, its governing structures dismantled, and large parts of the Palestinian territory have been destroyed.The leadership contest has exposed a widening strategic divide within Hamas over its future.Meshaal, who previously led the movement for decades while in exile, is associated with Hamas's traditional leadership.His supporters have argued that Hamas should draw lessons from the war, move away from a strategy centred primarily on armed confrontation, and adopt a more pragmatic political approach while preserving the movement's core objectives.However, supporters of Hayya successfully argued that Gaza's leadership, having borne the brunt of the conflict, should now lead the movement. They want to remain firm against pressure to make major concessions, particularly over the issue of disarmament.Since it agreed a ceasefire deal with Israel last October, Hamas has refused to give up its weapons without a guaranteed political path to Palestinian self-determination and statehood. It has rejected plans from the US-led Board of Peace that prioritise immediate demilitarisation over reconstruction.Hamas complains that there is a lack of security guarantees against continuing Israeli attacks and emerging rival armed groups.About 1,200 people were killed and 251 others were taken hostage in the 2023 Hamas-led attack on Israel.Israel responded by launching a massive military campaign in Gaza, during which more than 73,280 people have been killed, including 1,158 since the ceasefire began, according to the territory's Hamas-run health ministry.

schema.headline: Hamas names Khalil al-Hayya as new overall leader

title: Hosepipe ban extended to Surrey, Sussex, Hampshire and Berkshire
description: A hosepipe ban has been expanded to cover 2.4 million people across the south of England after record-breaking temperatures.South East Water's ban now
body: A hosepipe ban has been expanded to cover 2.4 million people across the south of England after record-breaking temperatures.South East Water's ban now includes parts of Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Berkshire - an extra 1.5m customers. A ban in parts of Kent has been in force since 3 July.Douglas Whitfield, SEW's water supply director, said it was "deeply sorry" and "incredibly grateful to everyone helping us protect water supplies" after exceptionally hot and dry weather.Eastbourne MP Josh Babarinde said he was "appalled", calling it "the direct result of years of underinvestment in upgrading infrastructure and building resilience".Under the restrictions, households in the affected area will be banned from using a hosepipe in their homes for tasks including cleaning, filling ponds, or watering plants.The ban will officially come in from 00:01 BST on Saturday, but SEW has asked its customers to abide by the restrictions immediately.The UK record for the hottest day in June was broken for a third day in a row last month, with temperatures reaching 37.3C, the Met Office said.

schema.headline: Hosepipe ban extended to Surrey, Sussex, Hampshire and Berkshire

title: Edward Stourton: Broadcaster and journalist dies aged 68
description: BySteven McIntoshEntertainment reporterBroadcaster and journalist Edward Stourton, whose career spanned more than 40 years including a decade on Radio
body: BySteven McIntoshEntertainment reporterBroadcaster and journalist Edward Stourton, whose career spanned more than 40 years including a decade on Radio 4's Today, has died aged 68. Stourton, who had been diagnosed with prostate cancer in 2014, died on Saturday surrounded by his family, the BBC announced in a statement. The presenter's friend and former Today co-host James Naughtie praised Stourton's "generosity of spirit", adding he was "a journalist of enormous capability and experience".Another former Today presenter, Evan Davis, said Stourton was "a very important figure for all of us on the daily news programmes on Radio 4"."One of those people you're really glad to have around," Davis reflected. "All substance, minimum ego, deeply knowledgeable, totally reliable."And he was one of the nicest of colleagues as well. He'll be missed."Presenter Mishal Husain described Stourton as "a true gentleman and a broadcaster of immense capacity and intellect", while Justin Webb remembered him as "funny, self-deprecating and unpompous".Figure caption, Jim Naughtie reflects on the life and legacy of broadcaster Edward Stourton, who has died aged 68.In his 2023 memoir Confessions, Stourton revealed he had been diagnosed with prostate cancer nearly a decade earlier.He had treatment but was later told it had become incurable and spread. Stourton continued to work until very recently.Stourton's career began at ITN, where he helped launch Channel 4 News and became the programme's first Washington correspondent in the latter years of the Reagan presidency.He joined the BBC as Paris correspondent in 1988, and after a period back at ITN as diplomatic editor, returned to present the One O'Clock News on BBC One. His BBC work also included Panorama, Assignment, Correspondent, as well as a phone-in programme on Radio 4, Call Ed Stourton.Stourton became a presenter of the flagship Today programme in 1999, where his immense experience as a foreign correspondent served him well while reporting from countries such as Lebanon and Afghanistan.He was also one of the first correspondents to reach New York after the 9/11 terror attacks.Image caption, Stourton in 2007 with Today co-hosts Carolyn Quinn, John Humphrys, Sarah Montague and James NaughtieHis departure from Today in 2008 was leaked to a newspaper before he had been told, upsetting Stourton and many of his colleagues.But he remained at the BBC and went on to present several other news programmes, while remaining as a contributor to Today.In recent years, as well as writing and presenting documentaries for Radio 4, Stourton fronted the network's religious affairs programme, Sunday, as well as regularly hosting The World At One and The World This Weekend.Image caption, Stourton's time at the BBC included working on Correspondent, which took him to KosovoStourton was born in 1957 in Nigeria, where his father was working, and also spent time as a boy in Geneva. He was educated at Ampleforth College and read English Literature at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he was President of the Union.His Catholic faith was an important part of his life and Stourton fronted documentaries including Absolute Truth, a four-part BBC Two series about the Catholic Church.It was also reflected in his writing - among Stourton's many books was John Paul II: Man of History, a revelatory biography of the former Pope. His last book, written to coincide with the 250th anniversary of the American Declaration of Independence, was Made in America: the Dark History that Led to Donald Trump.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Edward Stourton photographed at home in 2003Paying tribute, Naughtie praised Stourton's "great integrity and humanity", adding that he "had a kind of infectious decency which lifted people up"."He had a great effect on younger producers, who came away with enormous respect for the extent to which he tried to pass things on, and that he never patronised him,” Naughtie added."He was a man of wide cultural interests and experience and enthusiasm... His warm intelligence and wit were admired and enjoyed by generations with whom he worked."And as a friend, he was always a rock of support and kindness. His dignity and courage through his illness were an inspiration to everyone who knew him. He was, simply, much-loved."The Today programme's current editor Rebecca Keating said she and colleagues referred to Stourton as the "Rolls Royce of broadcasting"."His sharp mind and vast knowledge of the world brought to bear on any story with zero fuss," she wrote on X.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Stourton pictured at the Edinburgh International Book Festival at Charlotte Square Gardens in 2018The BBC's interim news chief Jonathan Munro said it was "far too soon to lose Ed"."I worked as his producer when he was ITN's diplomatic editor - unflappable then and ever after," he continued."Many years later, I was listening to his superb analysis of the new Pope for the BBC - another of his many areas of expertise. Not many broadcasters can present Today, BBC One's One O'clock News, The World at One and Sunday - but he did. He leaves a huge gap."Today presenter Nick Robinson said Stourton "brought a ferocious intellect, boundless curiosity and a good deal of charm to all he did". "He dealt with his cancer with resolution but without self pity," Robinson added. "He'll be badly missed."Radio 4 controller Mohit Bakaya described Stourton as an "absolutely outstanding broadcaster", adding that he "brought intelligence, authority and an extraordinary hinterland to everything he did".ITV's Julie Etchingham, who also presented Radio 4's Sunday programme, said she was "very sad to hear the news" of Stourton's death, remembering his "huge intellect and great heart".Stourton, who had homes in south London and south-west France, is survived by his wife Fiona, who is a senior television producer, two sons, a daughter and a step-daughter.

schema.headline: Edward Stourton: Broadcaster and journalist dies aged 68

title: World Cup 2026: BBC Sport delivers to fans in record numbers
description: As an incredible World Cup comes to an end, everyone here at the BBC wanted to say thank you.Thank you to the 42.8 million people who tuned in to our
body: As an incredible World Cup comes to an end, everyone here at the BBC wanted to say thank you.Thank you to the 42.8 million people who tuned in to our live match coverage on television or streamed games on iPlayer, the BBC Sport app and website 237 million times. That's a lot of football.Whether you were one of the 24 million who watched England v Argentina in the semi-final, the late-night 9.1 million who stayed up until 4am for England v Mexico, the 2.4 million who saw Scotland beat Haiti in the middle of the night, or part of the hardcore crew of half a million who gave Iran v New Zealand a chance at 2am and were rewarded with a four-goal thriller.Thank you to those of you who trusted us to not spoil your mornings, coming to iPlayer or the BBC Football YouTube channel every day for our eight-minute highlight packages. There were so many of you that we saw the biggest ever day and week in iPlayer's history.Thank you to the 57.2 million unique browsers who used our app and website 1.7 billion times, watching clips of our games, interviews and journalism, following our amazing live pages and reading stories from our journalists covering everything from the Trump-Fifa-Balogun scandal to tactical trends and the US entry denial of Somalian referee Omar Artan.

schema.headline: World Cup 2026: BBC Sport delivers to fans in record numbers

title: Hope Street: The small seaside town winning big from TV tourism
description: Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Donaghadee Harbour and lighthouse regularly feature in the show ByKevin SharkeyBBC News NIWhat happens to a p
body: Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Donaghadee Harbour and lighthouse regularly feature in the show ByKevin SharkeyBBC News NIWhat happens to a pretty seaside town when film crews descend upon it?People in "Port Devine" - with its multi-coloured houses, a lighthouse and spectacular coastal views - have been experiencing the effects for five years now.Its real name is Donaghadee and while its charm has been long appreciated by locals and day-trippers alike, the town's alter ego has captured the hearts of many more as home to the popular BBC police drama Hope Street.Fans have flocked to the County Down location since filming first started in 2021.Image caption, Kathryn Strain (left) and Bridie McElhill think the series has been good for their townKathryn Strain, who is a member of the Chunky Dunkers sea swimming group in Donaghadee, says it's common to see tourists from cruise ships visiting Belfast making trips to the town."You can hear them saying, 'oh, that's where the police station was, and oh, this is so and so's house'," so it has been great, it has been good for the town, definitely," she told BBC News NI.Image source, BBC/Long Story TV/Peter MarleyImage caption, Donaghadee's coastline featured prominently in the showSome of the Hope Street cast and crew have also braved the cold of Irish Sea and enjoyed - or maybe not - a dip.Like many locals, Kathryn can be spotted in Hope Street episodes as an extra."I think my most famous [part] was, and as my husband says I've got the face for a funeral, I was in it at a funeral," she jokes.Her friend, Bridie McElhill, who also swims with the Chunky Dunkers, says the show has helped put Donaghadee "on the map"."People come to Donaghadee now to see the town and see all the locations where the filming has taken place."I do think it has brought people into the town, and that's great for the town."Fans can also now own a piece of Hope Street history as a local charity shop is selling costumes from the show.A feature film - starring Finnian Garbutt who died in April from skin cancer - is due to be released in the autumn.Image source, BBC/Long Story TV/Peter MarleyImage caption, Finnian Garbutt played police constable Ryan Power in the police dramaAt the Stormy Cup cafe, staff had become accustomed to serving cast and crew members in recent years, but now they are catering for more tourists.Cafe manager, Brooke Pennick, says when Hope Street first came to town there was a buzz.Image caption, Cafe manager, Brooke Pennick, says having Hope Street filmed in the town was "special" "For locals, being able to see it happen right in front of their eyes, in front of their front doors, to crowds gathering down at the pier in the evenings to watch parts of that was such a special thing." Now, thanks to the show, she says more visitors are arriving in the town."We do have a lot of tourists coming here and wanting to see where things were filmed and go to proper sets and understand the logistics behind it."It's been wonderful, it's been something really special."Image caption, Sarah Cochrane manages Pier 36A local pub and restaurant was used as the fictional venue The Commodore in the drama.Sarah Cochrane, general manager of Pier 36, says people are coming to the town to find out more about sites and venues used for the drama."Over the summer we've had visitors from America, we've had Australian visitors that have watched the show."We've seen people from all over the place."One of Our Own - A Hope Street Film will air on the BBC this autumn following a cinema release across Northern Ireland in September, and further UK releases in October 2026.

schema.headline: Hope Street: The small seaside town winning big from TV tourism

title: Kevin Keegan: Liverpool legend and former Newcastle United and England manager dies aged 75
description: Former England captain and manager Kevin Keegan has died at the age of 75, seven weeks after he revealed he had been diagnosed with stage four cancer,
body: Former England captain and manager Kevin Keegan has died at the age of 75, seven weeks after he revealed he had been diagnosed with stage four cancer, his family have confirmed.One of English football's most decorated players, Keegan enjoyed spells at Scunthorpe United, Liverpool, Hamburg, Southampton and Newcastle during an illustrious playing career.He later went on to manage Newcastle, Fulham, England and Manchester City.Keegan revealed he had stage four cancer in June, which is the most advanced stage of cancer and means the disease has spread to other parts of the body.His family initially disclosed he was battling the disease in January, after he had "further evaluation of ongoing abdominal symptoms" in hospital.A statement on behalf of the Keegan family said he was "surrounded by his wife and daughters in his final moments"."Kevin, a double Ballon d'Or winner, was a much loved husband, father and grandfather," it continued."The family would like to thank Kevin's incredible medical team for all their support. This is a hugely difficult time and they are requesting space and privacy."Newcastle United paid tribute, describing Keegan as "one of the most iconic, influential and deeply loved figures in our club's history".The club statement added: "Kevin was more than a legendary footballer and manager. He was the beating heart of Newcastle United for generations of supporters."Kevin's impact will not be measured by appearances or results. It will live on in the memories he created with club and country, the dreams he inspired wherever he went, and the generations of supporters around the world who fell in love with football because of him."Liverpool, for whom Keegan played for between 1971 and 1977, said Keegan's "indomitable spirit and remarkable contribution will be forever etched in our history and his legacy will live on".Hamburg said football had lost one of its "defining figures" and they would always "cherish" his memory, while Fulham said Keegan "forged a strong connection" with the fans, who would "forever hold wonderful memories" of his short time at the club.England and Manchester City both offered their condolences to the Keegan family, with the latter describing him as "one of the most iconic and fondly remembered figures in English football".The Three Lions will celebrate Keegan's life when they host Spain in a Uefa Nations League match at Wembley on 26 September.As a player, Keegan won major honours with Liverpool, claiming three league titles, the European Cup, two Uefa Cups and the FA Cup over a six-year spell.He helped Hamburg win the Bundesliga and won back-to-back Ballons d'Or in 1978 and 1979. He remains one of only four English players to have won the award to date.On the international stage, Keegan scored 21 goals in 63 games for England between 1972 and 1982, and went on to manage them from February 1999 until October 2000.Keegan is one of the most transformational figures in Newcastle's history - the mastermind behind 'The Entertainers', who challenged for the Premier League title in 1996.Such is his status, current head coach Eddie Howe even reached out to Keegan when he got the Newcastle job in 2021 to try and get the inside track on the club and the mindset he needed to succeed.Keegan guided the club to promotion to the Premier League in 1992-93 before leading them to second place in 1995-96.He led England to Euro 2000 before resigning in October following a World Cup qualifying defeat by Germany at Wembley.Keegan managed City from 2001 to 2005, then returned to Newcastle for an eight-month spell in 2008.Former Newcastle striker Alan Shearer: "My hero. My manager. My friend. Sleep easy, Boss."Prince William, Patron of the Football Association: "Incredibly saddened to hear of the passing of Kevin Keegan. A truly remarkable footballer and manager who inspired generations with his talent, passion and love of the game. "Above all, he will be remembered as a kind and generous man. Our thoughts are with his family and all who knew him."Ex-Liverpool defender Jamie Carragher: "One of the greatest players this country has ever produced, trophies and goals galore, double Ballon d'Or winner shows he was an absolute giant of the English game."One of the most important men in making LFC what it is today."BBC Radio 5 Live senior football reporter Ian Dennis: "Really sad news. He had such a magnetic character. He was mesmeric as a player and a person. This is really sad news. Condolences to his family, friends and former colleagues."Everything he did, he put his heart and soul in to everything."Ex-Liverpool striker John Aldridge: "The great Kevin Keegan has passed away. He gave me great joy in my teens as a huge LFC fan, and the saying says never meet your heroes? Well I did! Roger Hunt and KK, and they surpassed everything I thought they were as people. Our thoughts are with all his family. God bless, Kevin. YNWA."Former Liverpool defender Mark Lawrenson: "He had time for everybody. You wouldn't say that he was completely gifted as a footballer but he worked like stink to get himself even better and quicker. He was such a lovely man. It's a very sad afternoon."Opposition supporters would actually clap when he came on the pitch which is very seldom to do."Ex-Newcastle striker Mick Quinn: "I knew Kevin didn't have long left and was getting a daily bulletin of his health in recent days."Sadly he has passed, it was a privilege to worship him as a kid playing for Liverpool then to get to work with him professionally and get to know him personally. R.I.P Kevin."Former Middlesbrough striker Jan Aage Fjortoft: "Kevin Keegan, one of the superstars of our game, has passed away. For us who discovered football in the 70s he was 'the one'."Later I had the pleasure to meet him, and he was such a nice and passionate guy. One part of his legacy will be that generations adored him regardless which team you supported. RIP."Ex-Tottenham centre-back Ramon Vega: "So sad. What a player and what a character as a manager! Kevin, you brought quality and greatness to the game. Thank you. R.I.P."

schema.headline: Kevin Keegan: Liverpool legend and former Newcastle United and England manager dies aged 75

title: World Cup 2026: Why David Beckham was face of the tournament in the US
description: In America, nobody sells football quite like David Beckham.During this World Cup, the former England captain has been impossible to miss.On the pitch,
body: In America, nobody sells football quite like David Beckham.During this World Cup, the former England captain has been impossible to miss.On the pitch, the tournament belongs to stars like Kylian Mbappe, Jude Bellingham, Lamine Yamal and Lionel Messi.Off the pitch, Sir David has become the face of the World Cup, fronting advertising campaigns for 11 brands ranging from McDonald's and Major League Soccer to Stella Artois, Home Depot and Bank of America.And if you haven't seen him during one of the many half-time commercial breaks, you've probably spotted him at the United States' opening match in Los Angeles, watching alongside his friend Tom Cruise.Just hours before that, Beckham had become the first professional footballer to receive a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame, with actor Cruise once again by his side.More recently, he was seen singing Wonderwall and Hey Jude as he celebrated Jude Bellingham's two goals against Norway that sent England into the semi-finals."David speaks to so many different people. I think that's what's unique about him," said Stacy Jones, founder and CEO of the marketing agency Hollywood Branded."He brings in the female demographic because he is so sexy - but he appeals to men. He's an honest, good, authentic, down-to-earth type of individual. So you don't really get affronted by him."She felt Beckham had achieved something few celebrities manage, moving effortlessly between football, fashion, entertainment, business and the cult of celebrity.On Hermosa Beach, south of Los Angeles, hundreds of fans gathered to watch Spain reach the World Cup final at an event organised by LA Galaxy - the club Beckham joined in 2007 from Real Madrid.Ask fans what comes to mind when they hear the name David Beckham and the answers come quickly."Bending it.""His free-kicks.""Victoria Beckham.""Handsome.""The Spice Girls.""The McDonald's advert.""David Beckham was a really good player for England, but he's doing too many commercials now," said one football fan.For Megan Hapeman, it's the recent McDonald's advert in which Beckham hopes to receive a cup featuring his own face.Her six-year-old son Oliver proudly told me he still has one."When I got it, I was pretty proud," he said. "Because Mommy liked it, I liked it too."That's exactly what brands are buying.Beckham transcends clubs, generations and even football itself.That broad appeal didn't happen by accident. It was built on Beckham's football legacy in America.He remains the player with the trademark free-kicks, part of Manchester United's Treble-winning side in 1999, England captain and one of Real Madrid's original Galácticos.Dan Courtemanche, Major League Soccer's executive vice-president and chief communications officer, admitted Beckham's influence on the game in America cannot be overstated."I always tell people he changed the fate of American soccer. Not once, but twice," he said.When Beckham signed for LA Galaxy 19 years ago, stadiums across Major League Soccer sold out before he had even played a competitive match.The league needed credibility and he gave it something even broader: mainstream celebrity.Sixteen years later, Beckham helped persuade Argentina icon Messi to choose Inter Miami over rival offers globally - another moment that transformed MLS, according to Courtemanche."Lionel Messi decided to make Major League Soccer his league of choice and Inter Miami his club of choice," he added. "Again, it took us to a whole other level."Tom Braun, LA Galaxy's president and chief operating officer, said Beckham's influence has stretched way beyond football."David's an iconic figure in our sport and our culture," he explained."He's built a business around his brand and continues to resonate with people."The evolution of the 51-year-old, who also played for AC Milan and Paris St-Germain before retiring in 2013, was perhaps best symbolised this summer on Hollywood Boulevard.When Beckham accepted his star on the Walk of Fame, he reflected he had never imagined "a working-class English footballer" would receive such an honour.The recognition celebrated not only the life of Beckham the footballer, but Beckham the cultural figure."David Beckham, to America, is the World Cup," Jones said."He is soccer to us."Having arrived in Los Angeles promising to grow the game, Beckham is still helping sell football to America.Only now, he's doing it not with a free-kick, but with a brand.

schema.headline: World Cup 2026: Why David Beckham was face of the tournament in the US

title: Key takeaways as Andy Burnham becomes PM
description: Image source, APUpdated 1 hour agoAndy Burnham has replaced Sir Keir Starmer as UK prime minister, after becoming Labour leader last week.The former m
body: Image source, APUpdated 1 hour agoAndy Burnham has replaced Sir Keir Starmer as UK prime minister, after becoming Labour leader last week.The former mayor of Greater Manchester becomes the country's fourth prime minister in five years and the seventh since 2016.Burnham says rough sleeping and the cost of living are prioritiesIn his first speech in Downing Street, Burnham announced he would "end rough sleeping in our country".This was swiftly followed by a No 10 press release promising an extra £340m for the task over the next five years. He also promised a new 10-year plan for Britain - to be unveiled later this year - and to build a new economy, putting "life's essentials back under public control".But arguing that people also needed "breathing space now", he said announcements on tackling the cost of living would start on Tuesday.Other changes he promised included changing the education system to help young people into work, building more council homes, and honouring commitments to fund defence while sticking to the UK's spending and debt rules.A new cabinet will soon be namedBurnham is expected to start making appointments to his cabinet on Monday afternoon, after sacking ministers so he can bring in his own top team.A big moment will be when we find out who his chancellor will be - Rachel Reeves has already confirmed she will be leaving government after turning down the offer of another cabinet role. Peter Kyle, David Lammy and Richard Hermer are among the Starmer cabinet ministers to also confirm they are leaving their jobs. Prime ministers tend to sack departing ministers behind closed doors, in the privacy of their House of Commons office, before those being invited to join the cabinet are then asked to walk up Downing Street, usually with a beaming smile.The new PM didn't use a lecternImage source, EPA/ShutterstockThere was much that was familiar about Burnham's speech in front of the famous black No 10 door, which was thronged with staffers and supporters. But perhaps conscious that "lectern moments" have become a symbol of the UK's political instability in recent years, Burnham broke with recent tradition and made the address without notes or a podium.He said that he was "acutely conscious" of the churn in the office behind him, stressing that the UK badly needed to regain stability.This would require politicians to "make politics work better" and "raise our game and rise to the new challenge", he added.Andy Burnham: Who is the new PM?From Manchester mayor to prime minister; what is his record and what does he stand for?Starmer and Burnham visited the KingKing Charles III said his farewells to Sir Keir in a final private audience at Buckingham Palace, before greeting Burnham.His role was central to the day because prime ministers are formally appointed by the sitting monarch, who is head of state.The short audience is known as "kissing hands" - although in practice, these days male premiers simply bow and shake hands with the monarch after accepting their offer to form a government.Image source, PA MediaNo hard feelings from StarmerEarlier, Sir Keir gave a short departure speech in Downing Street.With his supporters lining the street, he repeated key themes from the speech announcing his resignation in June, such as turning around the fortunes of the Labour Party and lowering child poverty.Despite leaving just two years after his landslide victory at the 2024 election, he insisted that he was proud of his record in office, saying Britain was "now stronger and fairer"."I go with good grace, I go with a smile," he said. He had time for a hug afterwards with Rachel Reeves, his chancellor and Downing Street neighbour during his time in office. Image source, EPA/ShutterstockGet in touchWhat questions do you have about the new PM and his policies?

schema.headline: Key takeaways as Andy Burnham becomes PM

title: World Cup 2026: Rodri's stock at all-time high as Spain midfielder adds to trophy collection
description: As Spain captain Rodri lifted the World Cup trophy high into the New Jersey sky, he added another trophy to his already glittering record.A Ballon d'O
body: As Spain captain Rodri lifted the World Cup trophy high into the New Jersey sky, he added another trophy to his already glittering record.A Ballon d'Or, one European title, one Nations League, four Premier League trophies, one Champions League and numerous other domestic triumphs. And now the greatest prize of them all.The Manchester City playmaker has long been revered for his club exploits, but now his stock is at an all-time high as he added world champion to his list of accolades when Spain beat Argentina 1-0 in the World Cup final on Sunday in New Jersey.Not only did the Spain captain steer his side to a first triumph since 2010 and stitch a second star on the national shirt, but he was also crowned player of the tournament.The 30-year-old becomes only the fourth men's player to win the World Cup, Euros, Champions League and Ballon d'Or, joining legends Gerd Muller, Franz Beckenbauer and Zinedine Zidane.But until recently, this level of success perhaps seemed out of reach for Rodri.After rupturing his anterior cruciate ligament in 2024, questions were asked about whether he could ever really return to the heights that saw him clinch the Treble with City in 2023 and claim the Euros with Spain a year later.Such a major injury takes time to recover from and it is only in glimpses during last season that we saw the Rodri of old.Now it seems the Spaniard was simply biding his time, patiently waiting for a full return to fitness in order to peak at the World Cup in the United States, Canada and Mexico.Rodri's future in Manchester remains uncertain with only one year left on his contract and Real Madrid reportedly coming knocking.It begs the question what next for Spain's conductor?"Rodri was absolutely outstanding," said Spanish football journalist Guillem Balague."He is not only the guy who ran the longest in the whole tournament and average per game, the amount of passes he gives to beat defenders, he is at his sharpest, he is at Ballon d'or level. This is Rodri's team."A second Ballon d'Or does not seem out of reach, although he has perhaps not played enough club football to make him the favourite for the individual accolade in a stacked field.Rodri is currently being linked with a move to Real Madrid, where returning manager Jose Mourinho is reportedly very keen on the composure, defensive ability and ball control of the former Atletico and Villarreal midfielder.New City boss Enzo Maresca is unlikely to be interested in letting Rodri go but, with only one year left on his contract, there is naturally a degree of uncertainty about the situation.One thing is for sure, his performance at this World Cup has placed him as a hot property - one that City would surely not want to part with but would also fetch a premium price if they did.Returning from an ACL injury is a notoriously difficult task.Having suffered the injury in September 2024, mere months after Spain's Euros triumph, Rodri missed the rest of that season then featured sporadically last campaign.He made 33 appearances across all competitions for City but suffered a number of injury setbacks and, at times, looked a shadow of the player he was before the injury.Former City boss Pep Guardiola predicted in October last year that we would not see the best of Rodri until the World Cup.And, as has so often been the case, the Catalan was right."I've gone through a logical process that every football player has to go through after an injury like this," Rodri said after Spain's triumph."It takes a year to get back to your best fitness level and then one more on top of that to feel like yourself again as the player you were. "You need to be patient, and you need to believe and be confident in yourself."It was a frank conversation with Guardiola, where Rodri told him he needed to stop to get his body right for the World Cup, that changed the trajectory of his recovery and ensured he was back to his best for the summer tournament."To get where I am today in my current form, I had to be clever," Rodri added."I had to know when to stop playing and when to carry on when I needed to, and that's part of what you need to do to recover properly."It's been a very tough time. I just want the new generations to see my example as an opportunity. "When you go down, you can rise again. This is my philosophy for my entire life."Of course, sometimes things go well, things go wrong, but always the positiveness."

schema.headline: World Cup 2026: Rodri's stock at all-time high as Spain midfielder adds to trophy collection

title: Chatsworth's parched grass reveals 'lost' 17th Century garden
description: The recent high temperatures have revealed the outline of an ornate and "lost" 17th Century garden at the Chatsworth Estate.The Great Parterre, which
body: The recent high temperatures have revealed the outline of an ornate and "lost" 17th Century garden at the Chatsworth Estate.The Great Parterre, which is beneath the south lawn at Chatsworth House in Derbyshire, was designed in 1699 for the 1st Duke of Devonshire, to complement the newly completed south facade of the house.This is not the first time the European-style formal garden has been exposed due to the hot and dry weather - it was also revealed during dry spells in 2022 and 2018.A spokesperson for the estate said after temperatures exceeded 30C in May, June and July, and with the "exceptionally dry" start to July, the lawn was "parched enough for history to reveal itself once again".

schema.headline: Chatsworth's parched grass reveals 'lost' 17th Century garden
